APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper targets neural dialog generation with conditional VAEs and introduces a knowledge-guided extension (kgCVAE) to capture discourse-level diversity, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The methodology (CVAE, latent variable z, bag-of-words loss and KL annealing), experiments on Switchboard (and LM comparison on PTB), and use of standard metrics (BLEU, embedding cosine, variational bound/perplexity) match the methods and evaluation standards expected at such venues. The paper explicitly addresses known challenges (vanishing latent variable, data requirements) and situates itself relative to prior work, indicating appropriate positioning for the community. | B(5): This work is squarely within top NLP/ML venues: it addresses neural dialog modeling (CVAE and kgCVAE) and discourse-level diversity, a mainstream NLP/ML topic. The methodology (conditional VAEs, KL annealing, bag-of-words loss), datasets (Switchboard, PTB), and evaluations (BLEU, embedding cosine, variational bound, perplexity) match community expectations. The paper targets the right audience and is positioned appropriately within neural dialog and generative-model research for a top conference.

The methodology (CVAE, latent variable z, bag-of-words loss and KL annealing), experiments on Switchboard (and LM comparison on PTB), and use of standard metrics (BLEU, embedding cosine, variational bound/perplexity) match the methods and evaluation standards expected at such venues. The paper explicitly addresses known challenges (vanishing latent variable, data requirements) and situates itself relative to prior work, indicating appropriate positioning for the community.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of generating discourse-level diverse dialog by introducing a CVAE with a latent variable z that yields diverse responses even with greedy decoding, and a knowledge-guided kgCVAE that jointly models explicit linguistic features (e.g., dialog acts y), which is a concrete advance for modeling high-level conversational decisions. The proposed training improvements (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) address the vanishing-latent-variable issue and the empirical results on Switchboard show substantially more diverse responses and discourse-level competence, making the approach likely to influence subsequent dialog-generation work. The contribution opens useful research directions (structured/knowledge-guided latent variables and improved VAE training for sequence generation) and is likely to be built upon, though its impact is tempered by noted training/data demands and the broader challenge of evaluating open-domain dialog. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem—generating discourse-level diverse responses in neural dialog—by introducing a CVAE that uses a latent variable z to produce diverse outputs even with greedy decoding and a kgCVAE that incorporates explicit linguistic features (e.g., dialog acts y) to guide generation. The proposed training remedies (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) tackle the vanishing latent-variable issue, and empirical results on Switchboard show substantially more diverse responses than an encoder–decoder baseline and evidence of discourse-level decision-making. These contributions are likely to change how researchers approach controllable, diverse dialog generation and to open follow-on work integrating explicit linguistic guidance and improved VAE training, making the work broadly useful to the community.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of generating discourse-level diverse dialog by introducing a CVAE with a latent variable z that yields diverse responses even with greedy decoding, and a knowledge-guided kgCVAE that jointly models explicit linguistic features (e.g., dialog acts y), which is a concrete advance for modeling high-level conversational decisions. The proposed training improvements (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) address the vanishing-latent-variable issue and the empirical results on Switchboard show substantially more diverse responses and discourse-level competence, making the approach likely to influence subsequent dialog-generation work. The contribution opens useful research directions (structured/knowledge-guided latent variables and improved VAE training for sequence generation) and is likely to be built upon, though its impact is tempered by noted training/data demands and the broader challenge of evaluating open-domain dialog.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—generating discourse-level diverse responses in neural dialog—by introducing a CVAE that uses a latent variable z to produce diverse outputs even with greedy decoding and a kgCVAE that incorporates explicit linguistic features (e.g., dialog acts y) to guide generation. The proposed training remedies (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) tackle the vanishing latent-variable issue, and empirical results on Switchboard show substantially more diverse responses than an encoder–decoder baseline and evidence of discourse-level decision-making. These contributions are likely to change how researchers approach controllable, diverse dialog generation and to open follow-on work integrating explicit linguistic guidance and improved VAE training, making the work broadly useful to the community.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are fairly comprehensive: the authors evaluate CVAE and kgCVAE on Switchboard (with a VAE LM comparison on Penn Treebank), compare against an encoder-decoder baseline, report multiple metrics (BLEU, sentence embedding cosine distance, variational lower bound, perplexity), and introduce and evaluate training fixes (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing). They discuss optimization challenges and negative results (vanishing latent variable, issues with word-drop decoding) and report hyperparameters, which aids reproducibility, while code availability is not mentioned. Related work is described as thorough. Missing elements that prevent a top score are limited dataset breadth (only SW and PTB) and no explicit mention of human evaluation or full ablation details in the summary, so some sensitivity analyses may be underreported. | B(4): The experiments are reasonably comprehensive: models are evaluated on a large conversational corpus (Switchboard) and a language modeling comparison on PTB, using multiple metrics (BLEU, embedding cosine, variational lower bound, perplexity) to support the diversity and modeling claims. The paper proposes and evaluates concrete training fixes (bag-of-words loss, KL annealing) but the summary does not make clear whether systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating each component were fully presented, so experimental analysis is strong but not exhaustively dissected. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and important limitations (training difficulty, vanishing latent variable, evaluation challenges) are explicitly discussed. Reproducibility is aided by reporting hyperparameters and using standard datasets, though code availability is not mentioned.

They discuss optimization challenges and negative results (vanishing latent variable, issues with word-drop decoding) and report hyperparameters, which aids reproducibility, while code availability is not mentioned. reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are reasonably comprehensive: models are evaluated on a large conversational corpus (Switchboard) and a language modeling comparison on PTB, using multiple metrics (BLEU, embedding cosine, variational lower bound, perplexity) to support the diversity and modeling claims. The paper proposes and evaluates concrete training fixes (bag-of-words loss, KL annealing) but the summary does not make clear whether systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating each component were fully presented, so experimental analysis is strong but not exhaustively dissected. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and important limitations (training difficulty, vanishing latent variable, evaluation challenges) are explicitly discussed. Reproducibility is aided by reporting hyperparameters and using standard datasets, though code availability is not mentioned.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares its CVAE and kgCVAE models against a standard encoder–decoder dialog baseline (similar to Serban et al., 2016a) and a VAE LM baseline on PTB, using the same datasets (Switchboard, PTB) and consistent metrics (smoothed sentence BLEU, cosine BOW embeddings, variational lower bound/perplexity). It also documents training issues (vanishing latent variable, data requirements) and reports reconstruction/selection metrics, and the summary notes thorough related-work coverage. The comparisons are therefore fair and appropriate, though the set of quantitative baselines appears somewhat limited (only an encoder–decoder and a VAE LM are reported), so some recent alternative diversity-promoting methods may be missing. | B(4): The paper compares to appropriate baselines: an encoder–decoder neural dialog model (similar to Serban et al., 2016a) for dialog and a standard VAE (Bowman et al., 2015) for LM experiments, using the same Switchboard data and common metrics (BLEU, embedding cosine distance). Differences are analyzed (CVAE vs. kgCVAE, reconstruction and training fixes like BOW loss and KL annealing) and the authors report thorough related-work coverage. However, the summary does not indicate explicit acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably or better, so comparisons appear good but with a minor omission in reporting relative performance nuances.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: a CVAE and a knowledge-guided CVAE (kgCVAE) are presented, training remedies (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) are proposed to mitigate the vanishing latent issue, and experiments on Switchboard (and PTB for VAE LM) show increased response diversity versus an encoder–decoder baseline. The methodological core (variational lower bound objective with the BoW augmentation) is standard and the summary reports appropriate baselines and metrics (BLEU, sentence-embedding cosine distance, perplexity), suggesting the mathematical and experimental treatments are sound. Minor concerns remain: no mention of human evaluation or statistical significance testing for the reported gains, and kgCVAE’s dependence on explicit linguistic features (y) may limit practical applicability if such annotations are unavailable. Overall the technical claims are well supported with only these modest limitations. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments on Switchboard and PTB showing improved diversity versus an encoder-decoder baseline and a VAE LM baseline, and by introducing kgCVAE plus training techniques (BoW loss, KL annealing) to mitigate the vanishing latent variable problem. The mathematical framework is standard (CVAE with ELBO augmented by BoW loss) and no incorrect derivations are indicated in the summary. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and datasets, though the summary does not report statistical significance testing and correctly notes limitations of automatic evaluation for open-domain dialog.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: a CVAE and a knowledge-guided CVAE (kgCVAE) are presented, training remedies (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) are proposed to mitigate the vanishing latent issue, and experiments on Switchboard (and PTB for VAE LM) show increased response diversity versus an encoder–decoder baseline. The methodological core (variational lower bound objective with the BoW augmentation) is standard and the summary reports appropriate baselines and metrics (BLEU, sentence-embedding cosine distance, perplexity), suggesting the mathematical and experimental treatments are sound. Minor concerns remain: no mention of human evaluation or statistical significance testing for the reported gains, and kgCVAE’s dependence on explicit linguistic features (y) may limit practical applicability if such annotations are unavailable. Overall the technical claims are well supported with only these modest limitations.

The mathematical framework is standard (CVAE with ELBO augmented by BoW loss) and no incorrect derivations are indicated in the summary. CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from model formulation (CVAE and kgCVAE) through training improvements to experiments on Switchboard and PTB. The main contributions (latent-variable dialog model, knowledge-guided extension, and training techniques like BOW loss and KL annealing) are stated early and summarized clearly. Notation and methodological descriptions seem consistent and well-defined given the detailed methodology in the summary; while figures/tables/examples are not enumerated in the summary, the reporting of multiple evaluation metrics and hyperparameters suggests informative experimental presentation, and the overall writing quality is described as good. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from methodology (CVAE and kgCVAE) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (latent z, kgCVAE with linguistic features, training fixes). Notation and methodological details seem consistently presented, and datasets (Switchboard, PTB), baselines, and hyperparameters are reported; writing quality is described as good and related work coverage thorough. The summary does not report on figures/tables or examples, so their effectiveness cannot be judged—this small uncertainty keeps the rating at 4 rather than 5.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a CVAE-based dialog model and a knowledge-guided extension (kgCVAE) that conditions on and jointly reconstructs explicit linguistic features y, which is a clear methodological extension tailored to discourse-level diversity. The proposed training remedies (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) directly address the vanishing latent variable problem with RNN decoders, showing non-trivial engineering and modeling adaptation. Empirical validation on Switchboard and a VAE LM comparison on PTB further differentiates the work from standard encoder-decoder and VAE baselines, indicating notable novelty rather than a purely incremental combination of known components. | B(4): The paper applies conditional variational autoencoders specifically to dialog generation and introduces a knowledge-guided CVAE (kgCVAE) that explicitly incorporates linguistic features (e.g., dialog acts y) to shape the latent discourse variable — a non-trivial extension beyond standard VAE/encoder–decoder work. It also proposes practical training remedies (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) to address the vanishing latent variable problem with RNN decoders. These aspects clearly differentiate the contributions from prior VAE and encoder–decoder dialog models and would be informative to experts, though they build on existing VAE ideas rather than introducing an entirely new paradigm.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a CVAE-based dialog model and a knowledge-guided extension (kgCVAE) that conditions on and jointly reconstructs explicit linguistic features y, which is a clear methodological extension tailored to discourse-level diversity. The proposed training remedies (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) directly address the vanishing latent variable problem with RNN decoders, showing non-trivial engineering and modeling adaptation. Empirical validation on Switchboard and a VAE LM comparison on PTB further differentiates the work from standard encoder-decoder and VAE baselines, indicating notable novelty rather than a purely incremental combination of known components.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies conditional variational autoencoders specifically to dialog generation and introduces a knowledge-guided CVAE (kgCVAE) that explicitly incorporates linguistic features (e.g., dialog acts y) to shape the latent discourse variable — a non-trivial extension beyond standard VAE/encoder–decoder work. It also proposes practical training remedies (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) to address the vanishing latent variable problem with RNN decoders. These aspects clearly differentiate the contributions from prior VAE and encoder–decoder dialog models and would be informative to experts, though they build on existing VAE ideas rather than introducing an entirely new paradigm.

main_claims: Introduce a CVAE-based neural dialog model that captures discourse-level diversity via a latent variable z, enabling generation of diverse responses even with greedy decoding.

Propose a knowledge-guided CVAE (kgCVAE) that incorporates explicit linguistic features (e.g., dialog acts y) to guide the latent variable and jointly reconstruct y, allowing the model to output high-level labels alongside word-level responses.
Develop training improvements (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) to mitigate optimization challenges such as the vanishing latent variable problem when using RNN decoders.

Empirically validate the models on the Switchboard corpus (and a VAE LM comparison on Penn Treebank), showing the proposed models generate significantly more diverse responses than an encoder-decoder baseline and exhibit discourse-level decision-making competence.
_summary.methodology: Adapt conditional variational autoencoders for dialog by introducing a latent variable z conditioned on context c (CVAE); extend to kgCVAE by conditioning generation on explicit linguistic features y and predicting y from z and c; train by maximizing a variational lower bound augmented with a bag-of-words loss and using KL annealing.
baselines: Encoder-decoder neural dialog model without latent variables (similar to Serban et al., 2016a)
Standard VAE (for LM comparison, per Bowman et al., 2015)
metrics: Cosine distance of bag-of-word sentence embeddings
Variational lower bound (used for model selection)
Perplexity / reconstruction perplexity (reported for LM comparisons)
limitations: Training CVAE is a challenging optimization problem and often requires a large amount of data.
Vanishing latent variable problem: straightforward VAE with RNN decoder tends to ignore the latent variable.
Automatic evaluation of open-domain generative dialog models remains an open research challenge.
Word drop decoding may hurt performance when the drop rate is too high, so it was not used.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - neural dialog / conversational models (CVAE-based generation)
